title: losetup fails for the loop device created by host after starting container
body:
### Issue Description

When a loop device is exported at the time of starting rootful container, `losetup` works fine inside the container. However, if a loop device is created by host using `nsenter` command after starting container, the `losetup` fails for the loop device creating runtime.

### Steps to reproduce the issue

Steps to reproduce the issue
1. Loop devices created on the host during OS boot
```
# ls -Zl /dev/loop100 /dev/loop400
brw-rw----. 1 root disk system_u:object_r:fixed_disk_device_t:s0 7, 100 Apr 16 15:11 /dev/loop100
brw-rw----. 1 root disk system_u:object_r:fixed_disk_device_t:s0 7, 400 Jan  5 13:53 /dev/loop400
```
2. Loop device /dev/loop100 is exported to rootful container during `podman run`. The container has privileges to call losetup and mount commands.

3. After the container starts
```
# ls -l /dev/loop*
brw-rw----. 1 root disk system_u:object_r:container_file_t:s0:c264 7, 100 Apr 16 15:09 /dev/loop100
```

4. Create a virtual block file, apply file-system, run losetup and mount using this loop device
```
# truncate -s 100m /tmp/a.img
# mkfs -t xfs /tmp/a.img
# mkdir /mnt/a
# losetup /dev/loop100 /tmp/a.img
# losetup -a
/dev/loop100: [0255]:1304753196 (/tmp/a.img)
# mount /tmp/a.img /mnt/a
# df -H | grep /mnt/a
/dev/loop100                                             100M  6.3M   94M   7% /mnt/a
```

5. Create a loop device from the host inside container
```
# nsenter -t 3746970 -m mknod -Z -m 660 /dev/loop400 b 7 400
# nsenter -t 3746970 -m chown root:disk /dev/loop400
```

6. Check the loop devices inside the container
```
brw-rw----. 1 root disk system_u:object_r:container_file_t:s0:c264 7, 100 Apr 16 15:09 /dev/loop100
brw-rw----. 1 root disk system_u:object_r:container_file_t:s0:c264 7, 400 Apr 16 15:15 /dev/loop400
```

7. Create another virtual block file, apply file-system, run losetup and mount using this loop device
```
# truncate -s 100m /tmp/b.img
# mkfs -t xfs /tmp/b.img
# mkdir /mnt/b
# losetup /dev/loop400 /tmp/b.img
losetup: /dev/loop400: failed to set up loop device: Operation not permitted
```
8. losetup output on host
```
# losetup
NAME         SIZELIMIT OFFSET AUTOCLEAR RO BACK-FILE                           DIO LOG-SEC
/dev/loop100         0      0         0  0 /tmp/a.img                            0     512
```

### Describe the results you received

losetup failed

### Describe the results you expected

losetup should succeed

### podman info output


### Privileged Or Rootless

None

### Upstream Latest Release

comments.body:
@rhatdan ,
This is in regard to Fibre Channel device discussion we had this morning in the Podman Community Cabal. Was trying to see any difference in devices presented at the time of starting container vs created using nsenter afterwards using loop devices.
@vikas-goel could you provide a reproducer for step 2?  also does strace reveal anything obvious to you?
Any AVCs in the logs? This feels like it could be SELinux. Sure way to check would be to try a container with `--label=disable`

On the host:
```
# stat /dev/loop500 /dev/loop501

Change: 2024-01-05 13:53:01.766290712 -0800

# podman run -dt --name mydevc --cap-add=CAP_SYS_ADMIN --security-opt=label=disable --security-opt=seccomp=unconfined --device /dev/loop500 myimg:latest bash
# nsenter -t 2409604 -m mknod -m 660 /dev/loop501 b 7 501
# nsenter -t 2409604 -m chown root:disk /dev/loop501
```

No SELinux AVC events for losetup on the host.
```
# ausearch -c losetup
<no matches>
#
```

This is most likely not an SELinux issue.  Try with `setenforce 0` to confirm.

I think this is something to do with namespaces.  @giuseppe WDYT?
Tried with `setenforce 0` on the host. No change in the result.
Means this is not SELinux.  Most likely this is a namespace issue.

could be a device cgroup configuration.

Tweak the configuration with `--device-cgroup-rule`.

In any case, I don't think this is a podman issue since the user is joining the namespaces using nsenter.

I am closing the issue, but feel free to comment further

I am not sure if device-cgroup-rule is going to have any role in this. In the hardened environment, containers are not given access to manage devices.

We should find root cause to make progress before closing the issue.

> I am not sure if device-cgroup-rule is going to have any role in this

have you tried it?

What I meant is the device-cgroup-rule alone won't help in my scenario as the containers don't have mknod capability.
The combination of `device-cgroup-rule` with `rw` mode and `nsenter` for `mknod` works, though. Thanks @giuseppe .
